          "beta particles, x-rays, and gamma rays), and therefore correlates with higher RBE. When"
        ],

        ]
      },

    {
      "kind": "replace",
      "left": {
        "start_index": 312,
        "end_index": 312,
        "start_page": 10,
        "end_page": 10,
        "lines": [
          "2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 10"
        ],
        "display_lines": [
          "[p010] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 10"
        ]
      },
      "right": {
        "start_index": 309,
        "end_index": 309,
        "start_page": 10,
        "end_page": 10,
        "lines": [
          "2026 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 10"
        ],

    },
    {
      "kind": "replace",
      "left": {
        "start_index": 337,
        "end_index": 345,
        "start_page": 11,
        "end_page": 11,
        "lines": [
          "On average, U.S. inhabitants receive a radiation dose of about 3.1 mSv each year. This dose",
          "comes from natural background radiation. Most of this background exposure comes from",
          "radon in the air, with smaller amounts from cosmic rays and the Earth itself. An additional",
          "2.2 mSv comes from man-made sources of radiation (mostly exposure from medical",
          "procedures, and some commercial and industrial sources). In general, a yearly dose of 5.3",
          "mSv from all radiation sources has not been shown to cause humans any harm. Figure 2",
          "breaks down sources of background radiation as discussed in NCRP report #184. Figure 3",
          "shows specific examples of common radiation exposures.",
          "2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 11"
        ],
        "display_lines": [
          "[p011] On average, U.S. inhabitants receive a radiation dose of about 3.1 mSv each year. This dose",
          "[p011] comes from natural background radiation. Most of this background exposure comes from",
          "[p011] radon in the air, with smaller amounts from cosmic rays and the Earth itself. An additional",
          "[p011] 2.2 mSv comes from man-made sources of radiation (mostly exposure from medical",
          "[p011] procedures, and some commercial and industrial sources). In general, a yearly dose of 5.3",
          "[p011] mSv from all radiation sources has not been shown to cause humans any harm. Figure 2",
          "[p011] breaks down sources of background radiation as discussed in NCRP report #184. Figure 3",
          "[p011] shows specific examples of common radiation exposures.",
          "[p011] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 11"
        ]
      },
      "right": {
        "start_index": 334,
        "end_index": 344,
        "start_page": 11,
        "end_page": 11,
        "lines": [
          "Collective dose is the sum of the individual doses received in a given period by a",
          "specified population from exposure to a specified source of radiation. On average,",
          "U.S. inhabitants receive a radiation dose of about 3.1 mSv each year. This dose comes",
          "from natural background radiation. Most of this background exposure comes from",
          "radon in the air, with smaller amounts from cosmic rays and the Earth itself. An",
          "additional 2.2 mSv comes from man-made sources of radiation (mostly exposure from",
          "medical procedures, and some commercial and industrial sources). In general, a yearly",
          "dose of 5.3 mSv from all radiation sources has not been shown to cause humans any",
          "harm. Figure 2 breaks down sources of background radiation as discussed in NCRP",
          "report #184. Figure 3 shows specific examples of common radiation exposures.",
          "2026 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 11"
        ],
        "display_lines": [
          "[p011] Collective dose is the sum of the individual doses received in a given period by a",
          "[p011] specified population from exposure to a specified source of radiation. On average,",
          "[p011] U.S. inhabitants receive a radiation dose of about 3.1 mSv each year. This dose comes",
          "[p011] from natural background radiation. Most of this background exposure comes from",
          "[p011] radon in the air, with smaller amounts from cosmic rays and the Earth itself. An",
          "[p011] additional 2.2 mSv comes from man-made sources of radiation (mostly exposure from",
          "[p011] medical procedures, and some commercial and industrial sources). In general, a yearly",
          "[p011] dose of 5.3 mSv from all radiation sources has not been shown to cause humans any",
          "[p011] harm. Figure 2 breaks down sources of background radiation as discussed in NCRP",
          "[p011] report #184. Figure 3 shows specific examples of common radiation exposures.",
          "[p011] 2026 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 11"
        ]
      }
    },
    {

    {
      "kind": "insert",
      "left": {
        "start_index": 380,
        "end_index": 379,
        "start_page": null,
        "end_page": null,
        "lines": [],
        "display_lines": []
      },
      "right": {
        "start_index": 379,
        "end_index": 381,
        "start_page": 14,
        "end_page": 14,
        "lines": [
          "Lethal dose (LD). The dose of radiation expected to cause death to 50 percent of an exposed",
          "population within a period of time (e.g. 30 days (LD 50/30)). Typically, the LD 50/30 is in",
          "the range from 400 to 450 rem (4 to 5 sieverts) received over a very short period."
        ],
        "display_lines": [
          "[p014] Lethal dose (LD). The dose of radiation expected to cause death to 50 percent of an exposed",
          "[p014] population within a period of time (e.g. 30 days (LD 50/30)). Typically, the LD 50/30 is in",
          "[p014] the range from 400 to 450 rem (4 to 5 sieverts) received over a very short period."
        ]
      }
    },
    {
      "kind": "replace",
      "left": {
        "start_index": 399,
        "end_index": 401,

          "Cataract (single acute) 5",
          "Cataract (fractionated) 8"
        ],
        "display_lines": [
          "[p014] Opacification (fractionated) 5",
          "[p014] Cataract (single acute) 5",
          "[p014] Cataract (fractionated) 8"
        ]
      },
      "right": {
        "start_index": 401,
        "end_index": 403,
        "start_page": 14,
        "end_page": 14,
        "lines": [
          "Opacification (fractionated) 0.5",
          "Cataract (single acute) 0.5",
          "2026 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 14"
        ],
        "display_lines": [
          "[p014] Opacification (fractionated) 0.5",

    {
      "kind": "replace",
      "left": {
        "start_index": 405,
        "end_index": 407,
        "start_page": 14,
        "end_page": 15,
        "lines": [
          "2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 14",
          "stochastic effects; this concept is called the linear no-threshold (LNT) model. The primary",
          "type of stochastic effect is carcinogenesis and genetic effects."
        ],
        "display_lines": [
          "[p014] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 14",
          "[p015] stochastic effects; this concept is called the linear no-threshold (LNT) model. The primary",
          "[p015] type of stochastic effect is carcinogenesis and genetic effects."
        ]
      },
      "right": {
        "start_index": 407,
        "end_index": 409,
        "start_page": 15,
        "end_page": 15,
        "lines": [
          "stochastic effects; this concept is called the linear no-threshold (LNT) model, which provides",
          "a conservative approach to radiation protection. The primary type of stochastic effect is",
          "carcinogenesis and genetic effects."
        ],
        "display_lines": [
          "[p015] stochastic effects; this concept is called the linear no-threshold (LNT) model, which provides",
          "[p015] a conservative approach to radiation protection. The primary type of stochastic effect is",
          "[p015] carcinogenesis and genetic effects."
        ]
      }
    },

      "right": {

    {
      "kind": "replace",
      "left": {
        "start_index": 528,
        "end_index": 536,
        "start_page": 18,
        "end_page": 18,
        "lines": [
          "Each sealed source must be tested at intervals not to exceed 6 months. In the absence of a",
          "certificate from a transferor that a test has been made within the 6 months before the transfer,",
          "the sealed source may not be used until tested. If a sealed source has leaked, a licensee shall",
          "file a report within 5 days if a required leak test reveals the presence of 185 Bq (0.005 μCi) or",
          "more of removable contamination. The report must be filed with the appropriate NRC",
          "Regional Office with a copy to the Director, Office of Nuclear Material Safety and",
          "Safeguards. The written report must include the model number and serial number, if assigned,",
          "of the leaking source, the radionuclide and its estimated activity, the results of the test, the",
          "date of the test, and the corrective action taken."
        ],
        "display_lines": [
          "[p018] Each sealed source must be tested at intervals not to exceed 6 months. In the absence of a",
          "[p018] certificate from a transferor that a test has been made within the 6 months before the transfer,",
          "[p018] the sealed source may not be used until tested. If a sealed source has leaked, a licensee shall",
          "[p018] file a report within 5 days if a required leak test reveals the presence of 185 Bq (0.005 μCi) or",
          "[p018] more of removable contamination. The report must be filed with the appropriate NRC",
          "[p018] Regional Office with a copy to the Director, Office of Nuclear Material Safety and",
          "[p018] Safeguards. The written report must include the model number and serial number, if assigned,",
          "[p018] of the leaking source, the radionuclide and its estimated activity, the results of the test, the",
          "[p018] date of the test, and the corrective action taken."
        ]
      },
      "right": {
        "start_index": 530,
        "end_index": 539,
        "start_page": 18,
        "end_page": 18,
        "lines": [
          "Each sealed source must undergo leak tests (wipe tested) at intervals not to exceed 6 months.",
          "In the absence of a certificate from a transferor that a test has been made within the 6 months",
          "before the transfer, the sealed source may not be used until tested. If a sealed source has",
          "leaked as determined by a wipe test, a licensee shall file a report within 5 days if a required",
          "leak test reveals the presence of 185 Bq (0.005 μCi) or more of removable contamination.",
          "The report must be filed with the appropriate NRC Regional Office with a copy to the",
          "Director, Office of Nuclear Material Safety and Safeguards. The written report must include",
          "the model number and serial number, if assigned, of the leaking source, the radionuclide and",
          "its estimated activity, the results of the test, the date of the wipe test, and the corrective action",
          "taken."
        ],
        "display_lines": [
          "[p018] Each sealed source must undergo leak tests (wipe tested) at intervals not to exceed 6 months.",
          "[p018] In the absence of a certificate from a transferor that a test has been made within the 6 months",
          "[p018] before the transfer, the sealed source may not be used until tested. If a sealed source has",
          "[p018] leaked as determined by a wipe test, a licensee shall file a report within 5 days if a required",
          "[p018] leak test reveals the presence of 185 Bq (0.005 μCi) or more of removable contamination.",
          "[p018] The report must be filed with the appropriate NRC Regional Office with a copy to the",
          "[p018] Director, Office of Nuclear Material Safety and Safeguards. The written report must include",
          "[p018] the model number and serial number, if assigned, of the leaking source, the radionuclide and",
          "[p018] its estimated activity, the results of the test, the date of the wipe test, and the corrective action",
          "[p018] taken."
        ]
      }
    },

      "kind": "replace",
      "left": {
        "start_index": 655,
        "end_index": 658,
        "start_page": 22,
        "end_page": 22,
        "lines": [
          "exposures. The NRC, in turn, maintains such radiation exposure data in its Radiation",
          "Exposure Information and Reporting System (REIRS). Thus, the REIRS database represents",
          "a resource for use in responding to workers' requests for exposure information and dose",
          "histories."
        ],
        "display_lines": [
          "[p022] exposures. The NRC, in turn, maintains such radiation exposure data in its Radiation",
          "[p022] Exposure Information and Reporting System (REIRS). Thus, the REIRS database represents",
          "[p022] a resource for use in responding to workers' requests for exposure information and dose",
          "[p022] histories."
        ]
      },
      "right": {
        "start_index": 658,
        "end_index": 661,
        "start_page": 22,
        "end_page": 22,
        "lines": [
          "dose equivalent exposures. The NRC, in turn, maintains such radiation exposure data in its",
          "Radiation Exposure Information and Reporting System (REIRS). Thus, the REIRS database",
          "represents a resource for use in responding to workers' requests for exposure information and",
          "dose histories."
        ],
        "display_lines": [

          "[p022] Radiation Exposure Information and Reporting System (REIRS). Thus, the REIRS database",
          "[p022] represents a resource for use in responding to workers' requests for exposure information and",
          "[p022] dose histories."
        ]

        ],

        ],

          "[p040] 2026 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 40"
        ]
      }
    },
    {
      "kind": "replace",
      "left": {
        "start_index": 1360,
        "end_index": 1360,
        "start_page": 41,
        "end_page": 41,
        "lines": [
          "elution will also vary."
        ],
        "display_lines": [
          "[p041] elution will also vary."
        ]
      },
      "right": {
        "start_index": 1362,
        "end_index": 1370,
        "start_page": 41,
        "end_page": 41,
        "lines": [
          "elution will also vary. A common type of 99Mo/99mTc-generator is the chromatographic one",
          "which is a convenient method to obtain a sterile, pyrogen-free and isotonic [99mTc] NaTcO",
          "eluate solution, which is ready for oral or intravenous administration or for aseptic production",
          "of 99mTc labeled radiopharmaceutical preparations. This type of 99Mo/99mTc-generator",
          "contains a sterile chromatographic column of glass with Al O material on which radioactive",
          "2 3",
          "99Mo is firmly fixed. The chromate-graphic column is surrounded by lead shielding. The",
          "99Mo/99mTc-generator has an additional bacteriological filter to ensure the sterility of the",
          "[99mTc] NaTcO eluate solution."
        ],
        "display_lines": [
          "[p041] elution will also vary. A common type of 99Mo/99mTc-generator is the chromatographic one",
          "[p041] which is a convenient method to obtain a sterile, pyrogen-free and isotonic [99mTc] NaTcO",
          "[p041] eluate solution, which is ready for oral or intravenous administration or for aseptic production",
          "[p041] of 99mTc labeled radiopharmaceutical preparations. This type of 99Mo/99mTc-generator",
          "[p041] contains a sterile chromatographic column of glass with Al O material on which radioactive",
          "[p041] 2 3",
          "[p041] 99Mo is firmly fixed. The chromate-graphic column is surrounded by lead shielding. The",
          "[p041] 99Mo/99mTc-generator has an additional bacteriological filter to ensure the sterility of the",
          "[p041] [99mTc] NaTcO eluate solution."
        ]
      }
    },
    {
      "kind": "insert",
      "left": {
        "start_index": 1363,
        "end_index": 1362,
        "start_page": null,
        "end_page": null,
        "lines": [],
        "display_lines": []
      },
      "right": {
        "start_index": 1373,
        "end_index": 1373,

        ],
        "display_lines": [
          "[p041] 2026 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 41"
        ]
      }
    },
    {
      "kind": "delete",

        "start_index": 1366,
        "end_index": 1366,
        "start_page": 41,

        "lines": [
          "2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 41"
        ],

        ]
      },
      "right": {
        "start_index": 1377,
        "end_index": 1376,

        "lines": [],

      }
    },
    {
      "kind": "replace",
      "left": {
        "start_index": 1387,
        "end_index": 1389,
        "start_page": 42,
        "end_page": 42,
        "lines": [
          "potentially causing an unnecessarily high radiation exposure to patients. However, no similar",
          "limit of breakthrough is specified for 68Ge/68Ga generators and applicants must commit",
          "individually to safety protocols to the NRC."
        ],
        "display_lines": [
          "[p042] potentially causing an unnecessarily high radiation exposure to patients. However, no similar",
          "[p042] limit of breakthrough is specified for 68Ge/68Ga generators and applicants must commit",
          "[p042] individually to safety protocols to the NRC."
        ]
      },
      "right": {
        "start_index": 1397,
        "end_index": 1401,
        "start_page": 42,
        "end_page": 42,
        "lines": [
          "potentially causing an unnecessarily high radiation exposure to patients. 99Mo has a 66 hr",
          "half-life and emits high energy γ photons and β particles and is tightly regulated to avoid",
          "unanticipated radiation dose to the patient. However, no similar limit of breakthrough is",
          "specified for 68Ge/68Ga generators and applicants must commit individually to safety",
          "protocols to the NRC."
        ],
        "display_lines": [
          "[p042] potentially causing an unnecessarily high radiation exposure to patients. 99Mo has a 66 hr",
          "[p042] half-life and emits high energy γ photons and β particles and is tightly regulated to avoid",
          "[p042] unanticipated radiation dose to the patient. However, no similar limit of breakthrough is",
          "[p042] specified for 68Ge/68Ga generators and applicants must commit individually to safety",
          "[p042] protocols to the NRC."
        ]
      }
    },
    {

    {
      "kind": "replace",
      "left": {
        "start_index": 1746,
        "end_index": 1750,
        "start_page": 53,
        "end_page": 53,
        "lines": [
          "sample changer for unattended counting of multiple samples. Because of their high intrinsic",
          "and geometric efficiencies (resulting from the use of a thick crystal and a well-type counting",
          "geometry, respectively), well counters are extremely sensitive and, in fact, can reliably be",
          "used only for counting activities up to approximately 37 kBq (0.001 mCi); at higher",
          "activities, dead-time counting losses become prohibitive and the measured counts, inaccurate."
        ],
        "display_lines": [
          "[p053] sample changer for unattended counting of multiple samples. Because of their high intrinsic",
          "[p053] and geometric efficiencies (resulting from the use of a thick crystal and a well-type counting",
          "[p053] geometry, respectively), well counters are extremely sensitive and, in fact, can reliably be",
          "[p053] used only for counting activities up to approximately 37 kBq (0.001 mCi); at higher",
          "[p053] activities, dead-time counting losses become prohibitive and the measured counts, inaccurate."
        ]
      },
      "right": {
        "start_index": 1758,
        "end_index": 1763,
        "start_page": 53,
        "end_page": 53,
        "lines": [
          "sample changer for unattended counting of multiple samples, and is able to determine the",
          "specific radionuclide contamination. Because of their high intrinsic and geometric",
          "efficiencies (resulting from the use of a thick crystal and a well-type counting geometry,",
          "respectively), well counters are extremely sensitive and, in fact, can reliably be used only for",
          "counting activities up to approximately 37 kBq (0.001 mCi); at higher activities, dead-time",
          "counting losses become prohibitive and the measured counts, inaccurate."
        ],
        "display_lines": [
          "[p053] sample changer for unattended counting of multiple samples, and is able to determine the",
          "[p053] specific radionuclide contamination. Because of their high intrinsic and geometric",
          "[p053] efficiencies (resulting from the use of a thick crystal and a well-type counting geometry,",
          "[p053] respectively), well counters are extremely sensitive and, in fact, can reliably be used only for",
          "[p053] counting activities up to approximately 37 kBq (0.001 mCi); at higher activities, dead-time",
          "[p053] counting losses become prohibitive and the measured counts, inaccurate."
        ]
      }
    },
    {
      "kind": "replace",
      "left": {
        "start_index": 1767,
        "end_index": 1773,
        "start_page": 53,
        "end_page": 53,
        "lines": [
          "Radio-thin-layer Chromatography (radio-TLC) is commonly used to analyze purity of",
          "radiopharmaceuticals or to determine the reaction conversion when optimizing radiosynthesis",
          "processes. In applications where there are few radioactive species, radio-TLC is preferred",
          "over radio-high-performance liquid chromatography due to its simplicity and relatively quick",
          "analysis time. Most approaches to radio-TLC were merely extensions or modifications of",
          "techniques that had been used in paper chromatography (PC).",
          "2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 53"
        ],
        "display_lines": [
          "[p053] Radio-thin-layer Chromatography (radio-TLC) is commonly used to analyze purity of",
          "[p053] radiopharmaceuticals or to determine the reaction conversion when optimizing radiosynthesis",
          "[p053] processes. In applications where there are few radioactive species, radio-TLC is preferred",
          "[p053] over radio-high-performance liquid chromatography due to its simplicity and relatively quick",
          "[p053] analysis time. Most approaches to radio-TLC were merely extensions or modifications of",
          "[p053] techniques that had been used in paper chromatography (PC).",
          "[p053] 2025 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 53"
        ]
      },
      "right": {
        "start_index": 1780,
        "end_index": 1787,
        "start_page": 53,
        "end_page": 54,
        "lines": [
          "Radio-thin-layer Chromatography (radio-TLC) is commonly used to analyze",
          "radiochemical purity of radiopharmaceuticals or to determine the reaction conversion when",
          "optimizing radiosynthesis processes. In applications where there are few radioactive species,",
          "radio-TLC is preferred over radio-high-performance liquid chromatography due to its",
          "2026 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 53",
          "simplicity and relatively quick analysis time. Most approaches to radio-TLC were merely",
          "extensions or modifications of techniques that had been used in paper chromatography (PC).",
          "2026 Radioisotope Safety Content (RISC) Study Guide 54"
        ],
        "display_lines": [
          "[p053] Radio-thin-layer Chromatography (radio-TLC) is commonly used to analyze",

          "[p053] radio-TLC is preferred over radio-high-performance liquid chromatography due to its",

1685[p050] • Acute high dose (>0.7 Gy) radiation exposure.1697[p050] • Acute high dose (>0.7 Gy) radiation exposure.
